full_response:
This problem is a review of the fundamental definition of matrix multiplication, which is a key mathematical tool used throughout physics, including special and general relativity. We are asked to write the product of two 3x3 matrices, [M] and [N], using the summation (Σ) notation.

**Step 1: Define the matrices and their elements**

Let [M] and [N] be two 3x3 square matrices. We denote the element in the i-th row and j-th column of [M] as M_ij, and similarly for [N] as N_ij. Here, the indices i (for rows) and j (for columns) run from 1 to 3.

The matrices are:
[M] =
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
M_{11} & M_{12} & M_{13} \\
M_{21} & M_{22} & M_{23} \\
M_{31} & M_{32} & M_{33}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

[N] =
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
N_{11} & N_{12} & N_{13} \\
N_{21} & N_{22} & N_{23} \\
N_{31} & N_{32} & N_{33}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Define the product matrix**

The product of [M] and [N], denoted as [P] = [M]·[N], is also a 3x3 matrix. Let's denote its elements by P_ij.

[P] =
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
P_{11} & P_{12} & P_{13} \\
P_{21} & P_{22} & P_{23} \\
P_{31} & P_{32} & P_{33}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: State the rule for matrix multiplication**

The fundamental rule for matrix multiplication is that the element P_ij in the i-th row and j-th column of the product matrix [P] is the dot product of the i-th row vector of the first matrix ([M]) and the j-th column vector of the second matrix ([N]).

**Step 4: Express an element of the product matrix as a sum**

Let's apply this rule to our 3x3 matrices. The i-th row of [M] is (M_i1, M_i2, M_i3). The j-th column of [N] is (N_1j, N_2j, N_3j).

The dot product of these two vectors gives the element P_ij:
P_ij = (M_i1)(N_1j) + (M_i2)(N_2j) + (M_i3)(N_3j)

**Step 5: Convert the sum to Σ notation**

The sum above can be written more compactly using the Σ (Sigma) notation. We introduce a summation index, let's call it k, which runs from 1 to 3. The expression for P_ij becomes:
P_ij = Σ_{k=1}^3 M_{ik} N_{kj}

This is the general formula for any element of the product matrix [M]·[N].

**Step 6: Write down the full product matrix using Σ notation**

The problem asks us to "write down [M]·[N]". This means we should express the entire resulting matrix, with each element written in the requested notation. We can do this by applying the formula for P_ij for each combination of i and j from 1 to 3.

For i=1, j=1: P₁₁ = Σ_{k=1}^3 M_{1k} N_{k1}
For i=1, j=2: P₁₂ = Σ_{k=1}^3 M_{1k} N_{k2}
...and so on for all nine elements.

The complete product matrix [M]·[N] is therefore:

[M]·[N] =
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k3} \\
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k3} \\
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k3}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

A more compact way to state this result is to define the product matrix by its components. If we let [P] = [M]·[N], the (i,j)-th component of the product matrix is given by:
P_ij = Σ_{k=1}^3 M_{ik} N_{kj}.

The matrix written above is the explicit representation requested.

\boxed{
\text{The product of the two 3x3 matrices [M] and [N] is another 3x3 matrix, whose element in the i-th row and j-th column, denoted by } ([M][N])_{ij}, \text{ is given by the formula:} \\
([M][N])_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{ik} N_{kj} \\
\text{Writing the full matrix explicitly:} \\
[M]·[N] =
\begin{pmatrix}
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k3} \\
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k3} \\
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k3}
\end{pmatrix}
}

Confidence: 100%
